sentences.base_language: Good morning. I have an interview at ten.
sentences.target_language: Buenos días. Tengo una entrevista a las diez.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Buenos días** = good morning — literally "good days" (plural). Compare: **buenas tardes** (good afternoon), **buenas noches** (good evening/night).
**Tengo una entrevista** — **tener** (to have) is used for appointments: **tener una cita** (to have an appointment), **tener una reunión** (to have a meeting).
**A las diez** = at ten o'clock.

sentences.base_language: Please sit down. Tell me about yourself.
sentences.target_language: Por favor, siéntese. Hábleme de usted.
sentences.grammarNote: Formal (usted) commands: siéntese (sit down), hábleme (tell me), espere (wait). For informal (tú): siéntate, háblame, espera. Use usted in formal settings like job interviews, with older people, or strangers in professional contexts.

sentences.base_language: I'm an engineer with five years of experience.
sentences.target_language: Soy ingeniero con cinco años de experiencia.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Soy ingeniero** — **ser** for profession, no article: **soy ingeniero**, **soy médico**, **soy abogada**.
**Ingeniero** from Latin **ingenium** (cleverness/talent) — same root as English "engine" and "ingenious".
**Con cinco años de experiencia** = with five years of experience — **de** links the noun (**años**) to what it describes (**experiencia**).

Use 'ser' for inherent character traits and professional identity: 'soy trabajador' (I am hardworking), 'es creativa' (she is creative). Use 'estar' for temporary states: 'estoy cansado' (I am tired today). Same adjective, different verb, different meaning.

**¿Cuáles son...?** — **cuáles** is the plural of **cuál** (which/what). Use **cuáles** when the answer will be multiple things.
**Sus** = your (formal, possessive for **usted**) — also means "his/her/their". Context (formal interview) clarifies it means "your".
**Principales** = main/principal (adjective, plural) — **principal** is invariable for gender: **el principal** / **la principal**.

sentences.base_language: I design and build software systems.
sentences.target_language: Diseño y construyo sistemas de software.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Diseño** = I design — **diseñar** is a regular -ar verb.
**Construyo** = I build — from **construir** (a -uir verb): **construyo, construyes, construye...** — the **y** is inserted before vowel endings.
**Sistemas de software** = software systems — **software** is an anglicism (borrowed word) used unchanged in Spanish.

sentences.base_language: The position is full-time with a good salary.
sentences.target_language: El puesto es a tiempo completo con un buen salario.
sentences.grammarNote:
**El puesto** = position/post — also: **el cargo** (role/position), **el trabajo** (job).
**A tiempo completo** = full-time — contrast with **a tiempo parcial** or **media jornada** (part-time).
**Buen** shortens before masculine singular nouns: **buen salario**, **buen trabajo**, **buen hombre**.
**Salario** from Latin **salarium** — the salt (**sal**) allowance given to Roman soldiers.

**¿Cuándo puede empezar?** — formal register using **usted** (so **puede**, not *puedes*).
**Poder + infinitive** = can/to be able to do something: **puede empezar** (you can start).
**Poder** is a stem-changing verb (o→ue): **puedo, puedes, puede, podemos, podéis, pueden**.

**Puedo empezar** = I can start — **poder** (o→ue) + infinitive.
**El próximo lunes** = next Monday — **próximo** (next/upcoming) before the day: **el próximo martes**, **la próxima semana**.
Alternatively: **el lunes que viene** (the Monday that comes) is equally common and colloquial.
